one_line_takeaway: LABSHIELD shows that strong MCQ safety-quiz accuracy in MLLMs does not transfer to reliable physical hazard perception and safety-aware planning in embodied lab settings, with even top models far below human performance and embodiment-specific training providing no consistent safety benefit.
key_finding: Across 25 evaluated models, no model approaches the human baseline (Human Mean MCQ 92.0%, S.Score 87.3%). The paper's headline number (from the abstract) is an average 32.0% drop from general-domain MCQ accuracy to Semi-open QA safety performance. Concretely: GPT-4o and Claude-4-Sonnet achieve identical MCQ accuracy (73.2%) but diverge sharply on Safety Score (41.7 vs 51.2), with Claude-4-Sonnet ahead on Unsafe Jaccard (33.0% vs 24.3%) and Hazard Jaccard (30.1% vs 21.5%), while GPT-4o shows a larger judge-vs-ground-truth 'hallucinated success' gap (Plan Score 78.4% vs Pass Rate 32.9%, a ~45.5 point gap) versus Claude-4-Sonnet's smaller gap (82.3% vs 41.5%). Reasoning-augmented models (GPT-o3, Gemini-3-Pro) show higher and more stable safety-critical accuracy and lower hazard-underestimation than non-reasoning peers, but remain far below human performance in high-risk (Safety L23) scenarios. The three embodiment-specific models evaluated (RoboBrain2.0-3b/32b, RoboBrain2.5-8b) do not show a consistent safety advantage over general-purpose MLLMs of comparable or smaller scale -- e.g., RoboBrain2.0-32b's S.Score (36.6) trails several general-purpose closed models such as Qwen-VL-Max (50.5) and GPT-5.2 (53.7). Ablations show multi-view input and higher image resolution both improve safety-relevant recognition (e.g., of GHS symbols and transparent glassware), and removing explicit safety-constraint prompting substantially degrades safety-related performance while in-context examples give only marginal MCQ gains. Attention-map visualizations show models disproportionately attend to high-contrast opaque objects while under-attending transparent glassware ('perceptual blindness'), which the authors link to downstream safety-reasoning failures.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LABSHIELD, a multimodal benchmark for evaluating whether multimodal large language model (MLLM) agents can safely perceive, reason about, and plan around physical hazards in autonomous scientific laboratories. It contributes: (1) a safety taxonomy grounded in OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1450 and the GHS chemical-hazard system, organized along a four-level operational hierarchy (L0 Atomic Action to L3 Mobile Manipulation) crossed with a four-tier safety taxonomy (S0 Harmless to S3 High-risk Violations mandating refusal); (2) 164 operational tasks with 1,439 VQA pairs collected via an Astribot robotic platform across three lab scenes (workbench, fume hood, sink) with four synchronized camera views (head, torso, left wrist, right wrist); and (3) a dual-track evaluation protocol -- Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) accuracy and semi-open QA scored on Perception/Reasoning/Planning sub-metrics -- applied to 33 MLLMs (20 proprietary, 9 open-source, 3 'embodied' models such as RoboBrain), with the main paper reporting 25 representative models.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not a security/adversarial-attack paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface defined. The paper's 'safety' concept concerns physical/operational hazard avoidance (fragile glassware, chemical exposure, GHS-labeled reagents, reagent incompatibility) by a well-intentioned embodied agent operating in a laboratory, not resistance to malicious inputs, prompt injection, jailbreaks, or an adversary trying to subvert the model. This is closer to embodied-AI safety/robotics reliability evaluation than to AI security.
limitations: Stated/implied within the paper's own text: (1) the dataset is comparatively small (164 tasks, 1,439 VQA pairs) versus some general-purpose embodied benchmarks it compares against in Table 1 (e.g., RoboBench at 6,092, ChemSafetyBench at 30k), though the paper argues its value is depth/safety-specificity rather than scale; (2) data are collected from a single robotic platform (Astribot) across only three lab scenes (workbench, sink, fume hood), which may limit generalization to other lab layouts/equipment; (3) semi-open QA scoring relies on an LLM-as-Judge (GPT-4o) for Plan Score and Analysis Score, which the authors themselves identify as prone to 'stochastic leniency' and 'hallucinated success' (motivating their Pass Rate regularization metric as a partial mitigation, not a full fix); (4) the full dataset is stated as 'will be released soon,' i.e., not yet publicly available at the time of writing; (5) the paper does not test robustness to adversarial or malicious instructions -- all failures analyzed are capability/perception failures of well-intentioned agents, not resistance to deliberately crafted attacks.

datasets_used: LABSHIELD (this paper's own benchmark): 164 operational tasks, 1,439 VQA pairs, 3 lab scenes (workbench, sink, fume hood), 4 camera views (head, torso, left wrist, right wrist), 4 operational levels (L0-L3) x 4 safety tiers (S0-S3), 14 unsafe-factor categories, 12 hazard-pattern categories, collected via the Astribot robotic platform
quantitative_results: Human baseline: MCQ Mean 92.0%, Semi-open S.Score 87.3%
GPT-4o: MCQ Mean 73.2%, S.Score 41.7%, U-J 24.3%, H-J 21.5%, Plan Sco. 78.4% vs Pass Rate 32.9%
Claude-4-Sonnet: MCQ Mean 73.2%, S.Score 51.2%, U-J 33.0%, H-J 30.1%, Plan Sco. 82.3% vs Pass Rate 41.5%
Overall abstract-level finding: average 32.0% drop from general-domain MCQ accuracy to Semi-open QA safety performance across evaluated models
baselines_compared: 33 MLLMs total (25 reported in main paper): OpenAI family (GPT-4o, GPT-5.2, GPT-o4-mini, GPT-o3), Google Gemini family (Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-3-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Flash), Anthropic Claude family (Claude4V-Sonnet, Claude4-Opus, Claude-4-Sonnet), HunYuan family, Qwen-VL Max/Qwen3 family, MiniMax, MoonShot-V1-8k, Seed-1.8, InternVL3/3.5 family (open-source), RoboBrain2.0/2.5 family (embodied), plus a domain-trained human annotator baseline
